name: Tunisia
tagline: From Carthage to the cradle of the Arab Spring
summary: Tunisia occupies the fertile hinge of the Mediterranean where the Phoenician city of Carthage once rivalled Rome. Across three thousand years the land was Punic, Roman, Christian, Vandal and Byzantine before the Arab conquest planted Islam and founded the holy city of Kairouan in 670. A succession of dynasties — Aghlabid, Fatimid, Zirid and Hafsid — made Ifriqiya a centre of learning before Ottoman governors and the Husaynid beys ruled from Tunis. France imposed a protectorate in 1881; Habib Bourguiba's Neo-Destour won independence in 1956; and in 2010–2011 the Jasmine Revolution overthrew Zine El Abidine Ben Ali and lit the fuse of the Arab Spring.

founding.label: Independence from France
founding.detail: On 20 March 1956 France recognised Tunisian sovereignty, ending seventy-five years of protectorate rule. Habib Bourguiba, leader of the Neo-Destour, became prime minister; in July 1957 the monarchy of the Husaynid beys was abolished and a republic proclaimed, with Bourguiba as its first president.

quickFacts.label: Carthage founded
quickFacts.value: traditionally 814 BCE, by Phoenicians from Tyre

quickFacts.label: Kairouan founded
quickFacts.value: 670 CE, first Arab-Muslim city of the Maghreb

eras.title: Carthage & the Punic Wars
eras.standfirst: Phoenician settlers built a maritime empire on the African coast — and fought Rome to the death for the Mediterranean.
body: According to tradition, Phoenician colonists from Tyre founded Carthage around 814 BCE on a promontory commanding the narrows between the eastern and western Mediterranean. From this harbour the Carthaginians — the Romans called them Poeni, hence 'Punic' — grew into a commercial power whose fleets, colonies and silver trade reached across North Africa, Sicily, Sardinia and into Iberia.

Carthage and the rising Roman Republic clashed in three Punic Wars between 264 and 146 BCE. In the Second War the Carthaginian general Hannibal led an army with war elephants across the Alps into Italy, winning crushing victories at Lake Trasimene and Cannae, before Scipio Africanus carried the war to Africa and defeated him at Zama in 202 BCE.

Half a century later Rome resolved to erase its old rival. In the spring of 146 BCE, after a long siege in the Third Punic War, Roman forces stormed and razed Carthage; survivors were sold into slavery and the territory became the Roman province of Africa. The destruction ended Phoenician power in the western Mediterranean.

events.title: Foundation of Carthage
events.summary: Phoenician settlers from Tyre establish Carthage, which grows into a dominant Mediterranean trading power.

events.title: Hannibal's war on Rome
events.summary: In the Second Punic War Hannibal invades Italy with elephants and wins at Cannae, before defeat at Zama in 202 BCE.

events.title: Rome destroys Carthage
events.summary: Roman armies raze Carthage in the Third Punic War; its lands become the province of Africa.

figures.name: Hannibal Barca
figures.blurb: Led Carthage in the Second Punic War, marching war elephants over the Alps to threaten Rome itself before his defeat at Zama.

pullquote.text: Carthago delenda est — Carthage must be destroyed.
pullquote.attribution: attributed to Cato the Elder, Roman senator

eras.title: Roman Africa, Christianity & the Vandals
eras.standfirst: Rebuilt by Rome, the province of Africa Proconsularis became a granary of the empire and a cradle of Latin Christianity, then passed to Vandals and Byzantines.
body: Julius Caesar's plan to refound Carthage was carried out by Augustus, and the city rose again as capital of Africa Proconsularis — among the wealthiest provinces of the Roman world, exporting grain and olive oil to feed Rome. Prosperity left lasting monuments, including the great amphitheatre of Thysdrus (El Djem), built around 238 CE to seat tens of thousands.

Roman Africa became one of the chief centres of early Latin Christianity. Carthage produced influential writers and bishops such as Tertullian and Cyprian in the second and third centuries, and the wider region shaped figures like Augustine of Hippo, whose theology profoundly influenced the Western Church.

As the Western Empire weakened, the Vandals under Gaiseric captured Carthage in 439 and made it the capital of a Germanic kingdom. The Eastern Roman (Byzantine) general Belisarius reconquered the province in 533–534, restoring imperial rule until the Arab armies arrived in the seventh century.

events.title: Augustus refounds Carthage
events.summary: Carthage is rebuilt as the capital of Africa Proconsularis, a prosperous grain-exporting Roman province.

events.title: Amphitheatre of El Djem
events.summary: The colossal amphitheatre of Thysdrus is built, the largest Roman amphitheatre in Africa and a later UNESCO World Heritage Site.

events.title: Vandals take Carthage
events.summary: Gaiseric's Vandals seize Carthage, making it the capital of a kingdom in North Africa.

events.title: Byzantine reconquest
events.summary: Belisarius restores Carthage to the Eastern Roman Empire, ending Vandal rule.

eras.title: Islam, Kairouan & the Medieval Dynasties
eras.standfirst: The Arab conquest brought Islam and founded Kairouan; under Aghlabids, Fatimids, Zirids and Hafsids, Ifriqiya became a centre of faith, learning and trade.
body: In 670 the Arab commander Uqba ibn Nafi founded Kairouan as a garrison and capital deep in the interior — the first Arab-Muslim city of the Maghreb and a holy city of Islam. Its Great Mosque became one of the most important religious monuments of the western Islamic world and a model for later North African mosques.

From their capital at Kairouan the Aghlabid emirs (800–909), nominally subject to the Abbasid caliphs, rebuilt the Great Mosque in stone and launched the conquest of Sicily. They were overthrown by the Shia Fatimids in 909; when the Fatimids moved their seat to Cairo in 972, they left their Zirid governors to rule Ifriqiya in their name.

After the Zirids broke with Cairo and the region was disrupted by Bedouin migrations, the Almohads and then the long-lived Hafsid dynasty (1229–1574) governed from Tunis, presiding over a prosperous era of Mediterranean trade and scholarship until the Ottomans absorbed the country in 1574.

events.title: Founding of Kairouan
events.summary: Uqba ibn Nafi establishes Kairouan with its Great Mosque, the first Arab-Muslim capital of the Maghreb.

events.title: Aghlabid dynasty
events.summary: The Aghlabids rule Ifriqiya from Kairouan, rebuild the Great Mosque in stone, and conquer Sicily.

events.title: Fatimid Caliphate
events.summary: The Shia Fatimids overthrow the Aghlabids; after moving to Cairo in 972 they leave the Zirids to govern locally.

events.title: Hafsid dynasty rules from Tunis
events.summary: The Hafsids make Tunis a prosperous capital of trade and learning until the Ottoman conquest.

figures.name: Uqba ibn Nafi
figures.blurb: Led the Arab advance into the Maghreb and founded Kairouan in 670, establishing Islam's first capital in the region.

eras.title: Ottoman Rule & the Husaynid Beys
eras.standfirst: Tunis became an autonomous Ottoman province governed, from 1705, by the Husaynid dynasty of beys ruling from Le Bardo.
body: The Ottomans took Tunis from Spanish forces in 1574 and made it a province of the empire, ruled by governors and the corps of janissaries while remaining a base for Mediterranean corsairs. Over time real power shifted to local rulers who governed with growing independence from Istanbul.

In 1705 Al-Husayn ibn Ali, an Ottoman officer, was proclaimed bey and founded the Husaynid dynasty, which would rule Tunisia until the republic of 1957. The beys conducted their own treaties with European powers and governed from the palaces of Le Bardo near Tunis.

In the nineteenth century the beylik tried to modernise — promulgating in 1861 the Arab world's first written constitution — but heavy borrowing left it bankrupt and exposed to European pressure, setting the stage for French intervention.

events.title: Ottoman conquest of Tunis
events.summary: The Ottomans take Tunis from Spain, making Tunisia an autonomous province of the empire.

events.title: Husaynid dynasty founded
events.summary: Al-Husayn ibn Ali is proclaimed bey, founding the dynasty that rules Tunisia until 1957.

events.title: First written constitution
events.summary: Tunisia adopts the Arab world's first written constitution amid attempts at modernising reform.

figures.name: Al-Husayn I ibn Ali
figures.blurb: An Ottoman officer proclaimed bey of Tunis in 1705; his line ruled Tunisia for two and a half centuries with growing autonomy from Istanbul.

eras.title: French Protectorate & Independence
eras.standfirst: France imposed a protectorate in 1881; a nationalist movement led by Habib Bourguiba's Neo-Destour won independence in 1956.
body: In 1881 a French army entered Tunisia and forced the bey to sign the Treaty of Bardo on 12 May, establishing a French protectorate; the Conventions of La Marsa of 1883 deepened French control. The beys remained on the throne, but real authority lay with the French Resident-General.

Opposition crystallised in the Destour (Constitution) Party and then, from 1934, in the more militant Neo-Destour founded by the young lawyer Habib Bourguiba. Through decades of agitation, imprisonment and negotiation, the movement built mass support for self-government.

France recognised Tunisian independence on 20 March 1956, and Bourguiba became prime minister. On 25 July 1957 the Constituent Assembly abolished the Husaynid monarchy and proclaimed a republic, with Bourguiba as its first president.

events.title: Treaty of Bardo
events.summary: The bey signs the Treaty of Bardo, placing Tunisia under a French protectorate.

events.title: Neo-Destour founded
events.summary: Habib Bourguiba and colleagues break from the Destour to form the Neo-Destour, the engine of the independence movement.

events.title: Tunisian independence
events.summary: France recognises Tunisian sovereignty; Bourguiba becomes prime minister.

events.title: Republic proclaimed
events.summary: The monarchy is abolished and Tunisia becomes a republic with Bourguiba as first president.

figures.name: Habib Bourguiba
figures.blurb: Founder of the Neo-Destour and architect of independence; as president he built a secular, modernising state and championed women's rights.

eras.title: The Republic & the Jasmine Revolution
eras.standfirst: Bourguiba's secular republic gave way to Ben Ali's long rule, until the 2010–2011 Jasmine Revolution ignited the Arab Spring.
body: As president, Bourguiba pursued an emphatically modernising, secular course. In 1956 his government promulgated the Code of Personal Status, which banned polygamy, raised the marriage age and expanded women's rights — among the most progressive family laws in the Arab world — and he later extended education and curbed the influence of religious courts.

In 1987 Prime Minister Zine El Abidine Ben Ali removed the ageing Bourguiba in a bloodless coup. Ben Ali ruled for twenty-three years over an increasingly repressive police state, marked by censorship, corruption and the suppression of dissent.

On 17 December 2010 the street vendor Mohamed Bouazizi set himself on fire in Sidi Bouzid, sparking nationwide protests. On 14 January 2011 Ben Ali fled to Saudi Arabia — the first Arab ruler toppled by a popular uprising — in what became known as the Jasmine Revolution. It inspired the wider Arab Spring, and Tunisia went on to a democratic transition widely regarded as the movement's one enduring success.

events.title: Code of Personal Status
events.summary: Bourguiba's government bans polygamy and expands women's rights, a landmark reform in the Arab world.

events.title: Ben Ali takes power
events.summary: Zine El Abidine Ben Ali removes Bourguiba in a bloodless coup, beginning twenty-three years of authoritarian rule.

events.title: Mohamed Bouazizi self-immolation
events.summary: A street vendor sets himself alight in Sidi Bouzid, triggering nationwide protests.

events.title: Jasmine Revolution ousts Ben Ali
events.summary: Ben Ali flees the country; the uprising sparks the wider Arab Spring and a Tunisian democratic transition.

figures.name: Mohamed Bouazizi
figures.role: Street vendor whose protest sparked the revolution
figures.blurb: His self-immolation in Sidi Bouzid in December 2010 ignited the Jasmine Revolution and, beyond Tunisia, the Arab Spring.

pullquote.text: Tunisia's revolution is widely considered the one Arab Spring uprising to have replaced an autocracy with a stable democracy.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on the Jasmine Revolution

figures.name: Hannibal Barca
figures.blurb: Carthage's greatest commander, who crossed the Alps with elephants to wage the Second Punic War against Rome.

figures.name: Habib Bourguiba
figures.blurb: Led Tunisia to independence in 1956 and built a secular, modernising republic noted for advancing women's rights.

figures.name: Mohamed Bouazizi
figures.blurb: His self-immolation in December 2010 set off the revolution that ended Ben Ali's rule and triggered the Arab Spring.

sources.label: Amphitheatre of El Jem — World Heritage List
Code of Personal Status in Tunisia
